citation: Mikhail D, Sugimoto D, Tadros M, Van Pelt R, Radel L, Soma D, Ulman S, Miller S, Jones J. Injury Differences Between Youth Male and Female American Football Players. The Physician and Sportsmedicine. 2025. DOI: 10.1080/00913847.2025.2468632
abstract: This descriptive epidemiological study analyzed 10 years of National Electronic Injury Surveillance System (NEISS) data (2014–2023) to compare injury patterns between youth male and female American football players ages 4–18. A total of 82,174 injuries were identified (95% male, 5% female; mean age 13.0 years). Males were more likely to sustain head (OR=1.32), knee (OR=1.23), and shoulder (OR=2.19) injuries and concussions (OR=1.50), while females had higher odds of finger (OR=3.22), ankle (OR=1.25), and sprain/strain injuries (OR=1.32). Top overall diagnoses were fractures (24.5%), sprains/strains (22.7%), contusions (12.2%), concussions (9.8%), and internal injuries (7.1%).
tldr: Youth male football players face significantly greater risk of concussions and head, knee, and shoulder injuries compared to female players, who are more prone to finger and ankle sprains — differences likely driven by greater male participation in tackle football versus female-dominant flag football. Staff working with female football athletes should prioritize ankle and finger injury prevention, while programs serving male youth players should maintain robust concussion surveillance and head/shoulder protection protocols.
methods: Retrospective descriptive epidemiological study using NEISS (National Electronic Injury Surveillance System) data over a 10-year period (January 1, 2014 – December 31, 2023). Patients ages 4–18 with American football-related injuries were included. Chi-square tests, odds ratios (OR) with 95% CI, and p-values (p<0.05) were used to compare top five injured body parts and diagnoses between sexes. Analyses performed in IBM SPSS v28.
findings: 82,174 total injuries identified; 95% male, 5% female. Most injured body parts: head (17.7%), finger (13.8%), knee (9.2%), shoulder (8.7%), ankle (8.0%). Most common diagnoses: fractures (24.5%), sprains/strains (22.7%), contusions (12.2%), concussions (9.8%), internal injuries (7.1%). Males significantly more likely to have head (OR=1.32), knee (OR=1.23), shoulder (OR=2.19) injuries and concussions (OR=1.50). Females significantly more likely to have finger (OR=3.22), ankle (OR=1.25) injuries and sprains/strains (OR=1.32). Fractures, contusions, and internal injuries did not significantly differ between sexes.
limitations: Female players comprised only 5% of the dataset, limiting statistical power for subgroup analyses. NEISS captures only emergency department visits, excluding sideline or clinical injuries. Football type (tackle vs. flag) and competitive level could not be differentiated. Potential recall bias from provider documentation. No longitudinal follow-up or outcome data included.
practicalImplications: Sex-specific injury prevention programs are warranted in youth football. Female football athletes — predominantly flag football players — need targeted ankle sprain and finger injury prevention protocols, while male tackle football programs should prioritize concussion prevention, head impact monitoring, and shoulder protection. Athletic trainers and team physicians should tailor their sideline assessment priorities based on the biological sex and football format of their athlete population.
athleteDev: Youth football programs should implement sex-specific injury screening and prevention curricula. For male tackle athletes, concussion education, proper tackling technique, and shoulder stability training are critical development priorities from a young age. For female flag football athletes, proprioceptive ankle training and hand/finger protection strategies should be integrated into off-season and in-season programming.
rtp: Return-to-play protocols for youth female football players should specifically address ankle ligament healing timelines and sprain severity grading, given their elevated sprain/strain risk. For male youth players, concussion return-to-play protocols must be strictly followed due to their elevated concussion risk. The elevated fracture rate overall in this young population underscores the need for clearance from orthopedic staff before return to contact activities.

citation: Platt B, Abed V, Khalily C, Sullivan B, Skinner M, Jacobs C, Johnson D, Stone AV. Injury rates remained elevated in the second National Football League season after the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. Arthroscopy, Sports Medicine, and Rehabilitation. 2023. DOI: 10.1016/j.asmr.2022.11.026
abstract: This cross-sectional study compared NFL injury incidence across the 2018–2021 seasons by querying publicly released weekly injury reports for players listed as 'out' or placed on injured reserve. Overall injury incidence in 2021 (28.70 per 1,000 exposures) was significantly elevated compared to pre-COVID 2018–2019 seasons (23.09/1,000; IRR=1.24) and the 2020 COVID season (21.64/1,000; IRR=1.33). Injury increases were observed across lower extremity, spine/core, upper extremity, and head anatomical zones, with the greatest relative increases seen mid-to-late season (weeks 5 and beyond). No positional group was disproportionately affected.
tldr: NFL injury rates in 2021 — the second season after COVID-19 onset — were higher than both pre-pandemic and pandemic seasons, with significant increases in mid-to-late season injuries across all anatomical zones. This suggests that the disruptions in training and preparation during COVID-19 had lasting effects that were not resolved within one season. For football performance and medical staffs, this underscores the long-term consequences of compressed or disrupted training periods on injury risk, and the critical importance of managing workload transitions carefully when returning athletes to full competition loads.
methods: Cross-sectional study using publicly available NFL weekly injury reports from fftoday.com and nfl.com covering 2018–2021 regular seasons and playoffs. Players listed as 'out' or on injured reserve for at least one game were included. Injuries categorized as lower extremity, upper extremity, spine/core, or head. Injury incidence calculated per 1,000 player exposures (one game = one exposure). Incidence rate ratios (IRR) calculated by dividing 2021 incidence by comparison cohort incidence. Z-test for proportions used for significance testing; Fisher's exact test for positional injury distribution differences. Bonferroni correction applied.
findings: Overall injury incidence in 2021: 28.70 per 1,000 exposures vs. 23.09 in 2018–2019 (IRR=1.24, p<0.001) and 21.64 in 2020 (IRR=1.33, p<0.001). Lower extremity injuries increased most in absolute terms (18.84 vs. 15.31/1,000 in 2018–2019; IRR=1.23). Head injuries showed highest relative increase (3.07 vs. 1.95/1,000 in 2018–2019; IRR=1.57). No significant positional differences in injury distribution. Injury rates were not significantly different in weeks 1–4 but were significantly elevated from week 5 onward in all comparisons.
limitations: Data sourced from publicly available injury reports rather than medical records, limiting injury detail and diagnostic accuracy. Only game-missed injuries were captured; sub-threshold or same-game-return injuries were not counted. Confounding variables across seasons (rule changes, expanded roster, additional game in 2021) difficult to fully control. Expanded IR return rules in 2020–2021 may have influenced IR listing patterns.
practicalImplications: When athlete populations experience significant disruptions to their normal training and competition schedules — analogous to COVID-19 impacts — sports medicine and performance staffs should anticipate delayed injury risk that emerges mid-to-late in the competitive season rather than immediately. Workload monitoring should incorporate an expanded chronic workload window after return-to-play periods, and progressive reloading programs should extend well beyond the initial weeks of competition to protect athletes through peak-season demands.
athleteDev: Extended disruptions to off-season and preseason training — whether from pandemics, lockouts, or other causes — create latent injury risk that manifests later in the season. Collegiate and professional football strength and conditioning programs should build periodization models that account for inadequate preparation time, using conservative acute-to-chronic workload ratios and extending physical preparation benchmarks before athletes reach full competitive loads.
rtp: Athletes returning from extended competitive layoffs (analogous to COVID-disrupted seasons) should not be considered fully protected from injury risk simply because early-season performance is unaffected. The mid-to-late season injury spike observed in this study suggests that functional return-to-play criteria should include assessments of cumulative workload tolerance, not just immediate performance readiness. RTP timelines following prolonged detraining should incorporate extended monitoring through weeks 5–10 of full competition exposure.

citation: O'Cain CM, Spratley EM, Arbogast KB, Lessley DJ, Evans WB, Cormier JM, Herzog MM, Weir N, Crandall JR, Coughlin MJ, Anderson RB. Injury Scenarios of Ankle Sprains in the National Football League: External Rotation and Eversion Mechanisms. The American Journal of Sports Medicine. 2025. DOI: 10.1177/03635465251329181
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study used video analysis to characterize 257 external rotation and eversion (EE) ankle sprains from 994 reported NFL game ankle sprains across four seasons (2017–2020). Eight standardized injury scenarios were identified: roll-up (44%), impact (25%), accordion (11%), change of direction (10%), crossed leg (5%), overload (2%), landing (2%), and misstep (1%). Direct player-to-player contact via a large external mass (opponent's pelvis or torso) accounted for 79% of injuries. Offensive linemen had the most sprains in absolute terms; running backs had 83% of their injuries from impact (tackle) scenarios. Only 15% of EE ankle sprains resulted from forces solely through the cleat-surface interaction.
tldr: The vast majority of external rotation/eversion (high/medial) ankle sprains in NFL players result from direct contact by a large external mass — typically an opponent's pelvis or torso — rather than from non-contact mechanisms or cleat-surface interaction alone. This means traditional bracing and cleat interventions will have limited effect on most EE ankle sprains, which require fundamentally different prevention strategies such as tackling technique modification for running backs and rule/education changes for linemen. Only the 15% of injuries occurring without direct external mass contact (common in safeties and cornerbacks) are well-suited to current bracing and proprioceptive training interventions.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology study. NFL Electronic Medical Record queried by IQVIA for missed-time ankle sprains over 4 seasons (2017–2020). Injuries with available video reviewed by 2 experienced biomechanical engineers who independently coded mechanism, player-to-player contact type, contacting body region, loading type, and injury scenario. Standardized terminology developed for 8 injury scenarios. Blinded matched-case comparisons (n=128 for mechanism; n=84 for scenario parameters) assessed reviewer agreement using percent agreement and Cohen kappa. Chi-square goodness-of-fit used to assess positional representativeness.
findings: 994 game ankle sprains reported; 383 with observable mechanism included in secondary review; 257 (67%) confirmed as EE mechanism. Offensive linemen had most sprains (n=52; 20%), followed by wide receivers (18%) and defensive linemen (14%). Rush plays most common (46%). Direct contact present in 75% of injuries; large external mass loading in 79%. Pelvis most common contacting body region (30%), followed by torso (26%). Roll-up most common scenario overall (44%); impact most common for WR, RB, QB. Running backs: 83% impact scenarios. Safeties and cornerbacks most commonly injured via change-of-direction (non-contact) scenarios. Reviewer agreement: mechanism 100%, scenario 95% (kappa=0.94).
limitations: Only 39% of reported ankle sprains had observable mechanisms due to video limitations, particularly for linemen who are less often the focus of broadcast footage. Play-type distribution may not be representative of overall game play due to the association between video availability and play type. Forces applied in injury scenarios were not quantified. The study was funded by NFL/NFLPA, which presents a potential conflict of interest.
practicalImplications: Ankle brace design and evaluation must account for the high-force, large-external-mass scenarios that cause most EE ankle sprains in football — standard braces tested against small positional changes may be inadequate. Prevention programs should differentiate by position: running back coaches should focus on tackling technique modifications; linemen programs should address body positioning to reduce roll-up scenarios; safeties and cornerbacks are the most appropriate population for bracing, proprioceptive training, and cleat-surface optimization. Spatting and taping may benefit players in low-force (small external mass) scenarios.
athleteDev: Understanding position-specific ankle injury mechanisms should drive sport-specific training design. Offensive and defensive linemen benefit from core stability and body position training to reduce roll-up exposure. Running backs benefit from ball-carrier contact preparation and lower extremity resilience training. Secondary players (CBs, safeties) should receive ankle proprioception and change-of-direction training with ankle protection protocols. These distinctions are directly applicable to position-group injury prevention programming at all competitive levels.
rtp: Return-to-play timelines for EE ankle sprains should reflect the mechanism of injury, as these injuries typically involve greater force loading than lateral sprains and result in longer recovery. Running backs injured via impact tackle scenarios and linemen injured via roll-up may require different RTP benchmarks, given distinct biomechanical demands. High ankle syndesmotic sprains resulting from EE mechanisms have well-documented extended recovery times and RTP criteria should include syndesmotic stress testing and functional loading assessments before clearance.

citation: Smoliga JM, Deshpande SK, Binney ZO. Interaction of Surface Type, Temperature, and Week of Season on Concussion Risk in the National Football League: A Bayesian Analysis. Epidemiology. 2023;34(6):807-816.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined 1,830 NFL games across 8 seasons (2012–2019) using Bayesian negative binomial regression to assess how playing surface, game temperature, and week of season — and their interactions — relate to diagnosed concussion risk. Artificial turf was associated with greater concussion risk than natural grass (IRR=0.78 for grass; >99% probability). Colder temperatures increased concussion risk (IRR=0.85 per 7.9°C decrease; >99% probability), and later season weeks independently increased risk (IRR=1.02 per week; >91% probability). Surface-by-temperature and surface-by-week interactions were also identified, with artificial turf concussion risk particularly amplified in cold weather.
tldr: Natural grass surfaces are associated with roughly 22% lower concussion risk than artificial turf in NFL games, and this protective effect is further diminished in cold weather and late in the season — factors that compound concussion risk multiplicatively. For football programs playing on artificial turf, especially late-season cold-weather games, concussion protocols should be heightened and medical staff should maintain increased vigilance. This study also raises important questions about season length policy, suggesting that extending NFL seasons likely increases cumulative concussion exposure.
methods: Retrospective cohort study of 8 NFL regular seasons (2012–2019). Concussion data sourced from PBS Frontline Concussion Watch and Football Outsiders injury databases. Playing surface categorized as natural grass or artificial turf (hybrid surfaces <5% excluded). Game temperature obtained from Pro-Football-Reference, official NFL game reports, and Weather Underground. Bayesian negative binomial regression models fitted with surface, temperature (z-scored), week of season, and surface×temperature and surface×week interaction terms. MCMC simulation via rstanarm (8 chains, 15,000 iterations). Incidence rate ratios (IRR) reported as median with 95% credible intervals. Sensitivity analyses with indoor-only and late-season-only models performed.
findings: 1,096 diagnosed concussions across 1,830 games. Natural grass associated with reduced concussion risk (IRR=0.78 [0.68, 0.89]; >99% probability). Concussion risk decreased with higher temperature (IRR=0.85 [0.76, 0.95] per 7.9°C; >99% probability). Concussion risk increased with each week of season (IRR=1.02 [1.00, 1.04]; >91% probability). Surface×temperature interaction: >84% probability of effect; artificial turf concussion risk particularly amplified in cold weather. Surface×week interaction: >75% probability of effect. Late-season (week 16) cold-weather (5°C) games on artificial turf predicted 0.96 concussions per game vs. 0.37 for early-season warm (25°C) grass games.
limitations: Concussions are likely underdiagnosed; analysis only captures diagnosed (reported) concussions. Player self-reporting biases may vary by surface, temperature, or season week. Considerable heterogeneity exists within 'artificial turf' and 'natural grass' categories not accounted for. Field wear and moisture data were not available. Playoff injury reports unavailable for eliminated teams, preventing playoff analysis. Study funded independently with no NFL funding but concussion diagnosis practices may reflect institutional biases.
practicalImplications: Football programs playing on artificial turf should implement heightened concussion surveillance protocols, particularly during cold-weather late-season games. Medical staffs should recognize that concussion risk compounds across the season regardless of surface, suggesting fatigue-related biological threshold changes require ongoing monitoring. Facilities with artificial turf may consider whether late-season high-stakes games warrant additional precautions. These findings also support NFLPA advocacy for transitioning to natural grass fields at the professional level.
athleteDev: The finding that concussion risk increases progressively over the season — beyond what temperature alone explains — has direct implications for athlete development periodization. Programs should consider incorporating cognitive load testing, neurological wellness monitoring, and potential practice intensity reductions in the second half of the competitive season. Exposure to cumulative sub-concussive impacts across a season may lower the concussion threshold for individual athletes, suggesting that protective measures should intensify as the season progresses.
rtp: Season-long accumulation of head impact exposure raises the possibility that a player's concussion threshold decreases progressively over the season, meaning later-season concussions may occur from lower-force events. Return-to-play protocols for late-season concussions should account for cumulative neurological exposure and not solely rely on acute symptom resolution. The interaction between surface type and cold weather further suggests that post-concussion return-to-play decisions should factor in upcoming playing conditions, with artificial turf cold-weather environments representing higher-risk return settings.

citation: Maestroni L, Turner A, Read P, Bettariga F, Fenu G, Rosalia A, Guastella M, Bishop C. Interlimb Asymmetry Data in Athletes With Anterior Cruciate Ligament Reconstruction: A Comparison of Different Equations to Interpret Between-Limb Difference Data. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research. 2025;00(00):1-9.
abstract: This study compared multiple asymmetry calculation equations in 53 amateur athletes (28.1 months post-ACL reconstruction) across unilateral strength and vertical jump tests and bilateral countermovement jump (CMJ). Large significant differences were found between unilateral equations (eta-squared 0.14–0.44) and bilateral equations for CMJ impulse asymmetries (0.17–0.34), with pairwise effect sizes of d=0.46–2.30 for unilateral tasks and d=0.99–3.04 for CMJ impulse. The choice of equation substantially altered whether an athlete would 'pass' or 'fail' a standard 10% asymmetry threshold, demonstrating that equation selection is a critical methodological decision in ACL rehabilitation and return-to-sport assessment.
tldr: The specific mathematical equation used to calculate interlimb asymmetry can dramatically change the magnitude, direction, and pass/fail outcome of a return-to-sport assessment — sometimes indicating opposite conclusions for the same athlete. Practitioners should adopt standardized, theoretically justified equations: the Standard Percentage Difference with direction for unilateral tasks (e.g., single-leg jumps, split squats) and the Bilateral Asymmetry Index 1 (BAI 1) for bilateral tasks (e.g., CMJ). This standardization is especially critical in football rehabilitation settings where asymmetry data drives return-to-play decisions and the 10% threshold is commonly applied.
methods: 53 amateur athletes (16 women, 37 men; mean 28.5 years; 28.1 months post-ACLR) completed strength and vertical jump testing: 5RM rear foot elevated split squat, 5RM leg extension, bilateral CMJ, single-leg CMJ, and single-leg drop jump using synchronized dual force plates (ForceDecks, Vald Performance). Five unilateral asymmetry equations (Limb Symmetry Index 2, Symmetry Index, Standard Percentage Difference, Symmetry Angle, Standard Percentage Difference with direction) and five bilateral equations (BSA, BAI 1, BAI 2, Symmetry Angle, BSA with direction) were compared. One-way ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis with eta-squared effect sizes, Bonferroni post hoc tests, and Cohen d pairwise effect sizes were calculated. Reliability assessed via ICC and CV.
findings: Most variables showed excellent reliability (ICC 0.925–0.978, CV 2.6–9.6%). Large significant differences between unilateral equations for all variables (eta-squared 0.14–0.44, all p<0.001). Large significant differences between bilateral equations for CMJ concentric and eccentric impulse (eta-squared 0.34 and 0.17). Pairwise differences between unilateral equations: d=0.46–2.30. Pairwise differences for CMJ impulse asymmetries: d=0.99–3.04. Case examples showed the same athlete could pass, fail, or return uncertain results depending solely on equation choice. Standard Percentage Difference with direction and BAI 1 identified as recommended equations for unilateral and bilateral tasks respectively.
limitations: Tests were limited to pushing-based movements; no pulling-based assessments (leg curl, Romanian deadlift) were included, potentially missing hamstring deficits common after semitendinosus-gracilis grafts. The study was conducted at a single private rehabilitation clinic in Italy, limiting generalizability. Interlimb asymmetries should not be used as the sole indicator of limb recovery or RTP readiness. The 10% threshold itself has questionable clinical validity.
practicalImplications: Football sports medicine and performance staff should standardize their asymmetry calculation methods immediately. Using inconsistent equations across assessments or between staff members can produce misleading data that either prematurely clears or unnecessarily delays athletes. The Standard Percentage Difference with direction is recommended for unilateral assessments (single-leg CMJ, hop tests, split squats) and BAI 1 for bilateral force plate assessments (bilateral CMJ impulse asymmetry). Teams using force plates for ACLR return-to-sport testing should audit their current equation use and align with recommended formulas.
athleteDev: For football athletes progressing through ACLR rehabilitation, asymmetry monitoring using consistent, appropriate equations enables more reliable tracking of limb recovery over time. As athletes approach return to full football activity, the Standard Percentage Difference with direction provides a stable denominator regardless of whether the ACLR or uninvolved limb is performing better — a scenario common in late-stage rehabilitation and return-to-sport phases when the reconstructed limb may exceed the uninvolved limb in some metrics.
rtp: Return-to-play decision-making for football players after ACL reconstruction is directly affected by the asymmetry equation chosen. Case examples in this study demonstrate that an athlete can pass with one equation and fail with another on the same test battery. Teams using the 10% asymmetry threshold for RTP clearance must specify which equation they are using and ensure it is the most appropriate for each task type. The BAI 1 is recommended for bilateral CMJ impulse asymmetry — a key metric in phase-specific RTP criteria — while the Standard Percentage Difference with direction should be used for all single-leg hop and strength assessments.
